title: Bottom-Up vs. Top-Down Pose Estimation
concept: Top-down pose estimation detects individual people first and then estimates their keypoints, whereas bottom-up detects all keypoints first and groups them.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a keypoint in pose estimation?

options.text: The pixel coordinates of a human joint, like an elbow or knee.
The border of the image frame.

exercises.explain: Correct! Keypoints represent the coordinate points of key joints across the body.

cards.title: A crowded dance floor
cards.body: If you are trying to map the poses of 50 dancers in a club, how do you do it? Do you look for individual people first and find their joints one-by-one? Or do you look at all the elbows and knees on the floor first and then try to connect them to the correct bodies?

cards.title: Two Core Approaches
cards.body: Top-Down Pose Estimation runs a person detector first, crops each person, and runs keypoint detection on each crop. Bottom-Up Pose Estimation detects all joint keypoints in the entire image at once, then groups them into individual skeletons.

cards.title: Sorting boxes vs. Dot-to-dot
cards.body: Top-down is like sorting toys: you put each action figure in its own cardboard box first, and then pose its arms inside the box. Bottom-up is like a massive dot-to-dot puzzle: you print all the dots on the page at once, and then draw lines to group them into characters.

cards.title: AlphaPose vs. OpenPose
cards.body: AlphaPose uses the top-down approach: if there are three people in the camera, it runs three separate keypoint models. OpenPose uses the bottom-up approach: it detects all knees and shoulders in the raw frame in one pass, regardless of how many people are there.

cards.title: Top-down is not always faster
cards.body: You might assume top-down is faster because it's intuitive. But if you have a crowded image with 100 people, a top-down model has to run keypoint inference 100 times! A bottom-up model runs inference once, making it much faster for dense crowds.

cards.title: Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up pipelines
cards.body: How the two architectures handle multi-person images:

left.text:
Detect people -> Crop -> Find joints
Accuracy: High (isolated crops)
Speed: Slows down as crowd grows

right.text:
Find all joints -> Group skeletons
Accuracy: Moderately high
Speed: Constant (independent of crowd)

exercises.prompt: Which pose estimation approach first detects person bounding boxes and then estimates keypoints inside those boxes?
exercises.explain: Top-down methods localize individual people first before locating their joints.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Bottom-up pose estimation becomes slower and slower as more people enter the frame.
exercises.explain: False. Bottom-up models detect all keypoints globally in a single forward pass, keeping speed constant regardless of crowd size.

exercises.sentence: While top-down starts with person detection, ___ pose estimation starts by detecting all keypoints globally.
exercises.explain: Bottom-up models find all joint keypoints first, then compile them into skeletal structures.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario represents a top-down pose estimation model at work?

options.text: Running YOLO to detect 5 hikers, cropping them, and running a keypoint model on each hiker
Predicting all 50 elbows on a marathon track first, then grouping them using limb directions
Classifying if a video represents walking or crawling

exercises.explain: Detecting human boundaries first and then running joint models inside those boundaries is top-down.

exercises.prompt: You are building a real-time crowd safety monitor for a stadium concourse that handles thousands of people simultaneously. Which pose estimation architecture should you choose and why?

options.text: Bottom-up pose estimation, because its processing time is constant, meaning it will not slow down or lag when massive crowds appear.
Top-down pose estimation, because running a crop-and-detect loop on 2,000 individuals is faster on standard mobile CPUs.
An image classification model, because you do not need coordinates to count people.
A facial verification model, to check the ticket ID of every spectator.

exercises.explain: Exactly! In dense crowds, a top-down model would grind to a halt because it scales linearly with the number of people. Bottom-up models maintain constant speed by analyzing the entire frame at once. Great job! Next: how models predict coordinates using heatmaps.
